schools.summary: After the riot subsided, Paul embraced the Ephesian disciples and departed for Macedonia — an affectionate leave-taking, continuing the third journey; pastoral care even in departure, not fleeing in haste but gathering and embracing the disciples.

themes: embraced them, and departed for Macedonia
the close of the Ephesian ministry

schools.summary: Paul went through Macedonia, exhorting the churches, and came into Greece (Achaia, particularly Corinth) — a period filled, from the epistles, with the meeting of Titus, the writing of 2 Corinthians, and labor as far as Illyricum (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott); diligent ministry, not merely traveling but strengthening the churches.

themes: gone over those parts, and given much exhortation
the rich period filled out by the epistles

schools.summary: Paul abode three months in Greece (probably writing Romans, Barnes, Robertson); when the Corinthian Jews — his old enemies from the Gallio affair (Robertson) — plotted to kill him as he was about to sail, he changed his route, returning through Macedonia.
Calvin: Paul's prudence — avoiding the ambush, having due regard for life without throwing himself headlong into danger; the balance of courage and prudence.

themes: the Jews laid wait for him
he purposed to return through Macedonia

schools.summary: A company of delegates accompanied Paul — Sopater, Aristarchus, Secundus, Gaius, Timothy, Tychicus, Trophimus — representatives of the churches carrying the collection for Jerusalem (Ellicott), that Paul might avoid any suspicion in handling the large sum (2 Corinthians 8:20-21); from the provinces that shared in the collection (Robertson).

The delegates went ahead to Troas and waited, while the 'we' narrative resumes — Luke rejoining Paul (last seen together at Philippi in ch. 16, Robertson), now to remain till Rome; the advance party would announce Paul's coming and prepare a full gathering (Ellicott).

themes: these going before tarried for us at Troas

schools.summary: Paul, having kept the Passover at Philippi (observing the Jewish feast though a Christian, Robertson), sailed to Troas in five days (slowed by contrary winds, Ellicott) and stayed seven days — the stay allowing him to keep the Lord's Day with the church and break bread (Ellicott).

themes: after the days of unleavened bread
came to Troas in five days
keeping the Passover and waiting for the Lord's Day

schools.summary: On the first day of the week the disciples gathered to break bread, and Paul preached till midnight — key evidence for Christian worship on Sunday; the 'breaking of bread' the Lord's Supper with the agape (Ellicott, Robertson, 1 Corinthians 10:16).

Henry: the first day called the Lord's day (Revelation 1:10), the Christian sabbath, celebrated in remembrance of the resurrection and the pouring out of the Spirit, both on the first day of the week.

debates.question: What does the 'first day of the week' gathering establish?

sides.position: Christian worship on Sunday — the Lord's Day, the Christian sabbath, in remembrance of the resurrection

themes: upon the first day of the week
the Lord's Day and Christian worship

schools.summary: Many lights in the upper chamber — mentioned partly to refute the slander that Christians met in darkness for wickedness (Barnes, Wesley), partly to account for Eutychus's sleep by the heat and closeness (Ellicott, Robertson); testifying to the openness and purity of the assembly.

themes: many lights in the upper chamber
refuting the slander of dark meetings
the heat accounting for Eutychus's sleep

schools.summary: Eutychus, overcome by deep sleep, fell from the third floor and was taken up dead; most hold a real death and miraculous restoration (Barnes; Robertson noting Luke the physician's 'dead' with no 'as if'); Ellicott leaves room for apparent death (a swoon from the shock), citing 'his life is in him.'
Calvin: defends Eutychus from the charge of culpable drowsiness — overcome by nature after midnight, not slothful; affirms he truly died, the Lord using it to confirm the faith.

debates.question: Did Eutychus truly die or only swoon?

sides.position: He truly died and was miraculously restored — Luke the physician's 'taken up dead'
Possibly only apparent death (a swoon from the fall's shock) — 'his life is in him'

themes: a young man named Eutychus, fallen into a deep sleep
fell down from the third loft, and was taken up dead

schools.summary: Paul went down, fell on Eutychus (as Elijah and Elisha, 1 Kings 17:21; 2 Kings 4:34), embraced him, and declared 'his life is in him' — restoring him to life through prayer (Barnes); the phrase could suit either a restoration or discovery he yet lived (Robertson).

Calvin: 'his soul is in him' does not deny the death but means his life was restored by God's grace; Paul's purpose, lest the event shake the church's faith — the Lord sealing Paul's last Troas sermon with the miracle.

themes: Paul fell on him, and embracing him
the manner of Elijah and Elisha

schools.summary: After the miracle, Paul broke bread and ate and talked till daybreak, then departed; the 'breaking of bread' probably the Eucharist (Robertson, Ellicott — the Supper interrupted, now observed) or a refreshing meal (Barnes); the 'talking' a more familiar conversation than the earlier solemn discourse (Clarke, Robertson).

themes: talked a long while, till break of day
the Supper resumed after the miracle

schools.summary: They brought the young man alive, and were greatly comforted (the litotes 'not a little' marking a great comfort, Barnes); the emphasis on 'alive' confirming he had been dead (Robertson); the double comfort of the youth's life and the confirmation of a miracle.

themes: they brought the young man alive
the joyful close of the incident

schools.summary: The companions sailed to Assos while Paul went on foot, intending to rejoin them — by his own appointment (Henry); the reasons conjectured: perhaps for solitude and prayer, perhaps to visit disciples, perhaps to avoid the sea passage; the apostle deliberately arranging his journey.

themes: we went before to ship, and sailed unto Assos

schools.summary: Paul met the ship at Assos (as arranged, having walked from Troas, Robertson), and they took him aboard and came to Mitylene (the capital of Lesbos, an easy day's sail); the resumption of the joint journey, the company reunited and sailing on.

themes: he met with us at Assos

schools.summary: The ship sailed down the coast — past Chios, to Samos, Trogyllium, and Miletus — the day-by-day progress of Mediterranean navigation, anchoring each night (Ellicott); Miletus the port near Ephesus, once chief of the Ionian cities but now eclipsed by Ephesus (Robertson), where Paul would summon the elders.

themes: came over against Chios, arrived at Samos
arrival at the scene of the farewell

schools.summary: Paul determined to sail past Ephesus (not stopping, to avoid delay) because he hastened to Jerusalem for Pentecost — the feast that gathered the most pilgrims, giving scope for his work and the presentation of the collection (Ellicott); by summoning the elders to Miletus he could embark at any moment (Ellicott).
Calvin: Paul's motive was not legal observance of the feast but the edifying of the church — the enlarging of Christ's kingdom, the gaining of strangers, the silencing of slander.

themes: hasted to be at Jerusalem the day of Pentecost
passing by Ephesus to avoid delay
the reason for the Miletus meeting

schools.summary: Paul, from Miletus, sent for the elders of the Ephesian church (about thirty miles away) — not overlooking them despite passing by Ephesus, but taking order for all (Chrysostom), conveying his charge to the whole church through its leaders; the elders the same called 'bishops' in v.28 (the identity of elder and bishop, Calvin).

themes: from Miletus he sent to Ephesus
called the elders of the church
summoning the elders despite passing Ephesus
the convening of the farewell address

schools.summary: Paul opened by appealing to the elders' own knowledge of his manner of life from the first day in Asia — not boasting but, with humility, calling them as witnesses (Chrysostom), setting his example as a pattern to follow.
Calvin: Paul sets his own example before the elders, that the pattern of ministry might always be before their eyes for imitation.

themes: ye know after what manner I have been with you

schools.summary: Paul served the Lord with humility (the groundwork of all good in a ruler, Chrysostom), with many tears (his affectionate concern), and amid temptations from Jewish plots — the model of the faithful minister: humble, tearful, and steadfast under persecution.

themes: serving the Lord with all humility of mind

schools.summary: Paul kept back nothing profitable but taught fully, publicly and house to house — ungrudging fullness joined to wise discretion (all that was profitable, not folly, Chrysostom), with great toil, reaching both the assembly and individuals.
Calvin: pastors must deliver all that Scripture reveals, plainly and without guile — Paul withholding nothing profitable, teaching the whole counsel of God both publicly and privately.

themes: kept back nothing that was profitable
taught publickly, and from house to house

schools.summary: Paul testified to both Jews and Greeks (no distinction, Chrysostom) the twofold sum of the gospel — repentance toward God and faith toward the Lord Jesus Christ; the two essential, inseparable responses: repentance for sin and faith for salvation (Barnes).

themes: testifying to the Jews and also to the Greeks
repentance toward God, and faith toward Christ
the twofold sum of the gospel

schools.summary: Paul went 'bound in the spirit' to Jerusalem; Ellicott and Robertson favor Paul's own spirit under the Spirit's control (Romans 8:16) — the sense not greatly differing; he knew bonds awaited but not the particular outcome.
Gill, Barnes, Clarke, Calvin favor the Holy Spirit's compulsion — the inward force and motion of the Spirit; Calvin: Paul spoke doubtfully at first to soften the bitter news of what awaited.

debates.question: Is 'bound in the spirit' Paul's own spirit or the Holy Spirit?

sides.position: Paul's own spirit, under the Spirit's control (the sense not greatly differing)

themes: I go bound in the spirit unto Jerusalem
not knowing the things that shall befall me
Paul's spirit or the Holy Spirit

schools.summary: The Holy Spirit testified in every city, through the prophets (as Agabus, 21:11), that bonds and afflictions awaited Paul — the specific mention of the Holy Spirit contrasting with Paul's own spirit in v.22 (Robertson); Ellicott's lesson: the prophets foresaw truly yet were not infallible advisers.

themes: the Holy Ghost witnesseth in every city

schools.summary: Paul declared 'none of these things move me' — undeterred by the foretold bonds (like Luther at Worms, Ellicott), counting not his life dear, valuing duty above life (Barnes), desiring only to finish his course and testify the gospel of grace; 'with joy' textually doubtful, perhaps a later insertion (Ellicott, Clarke).

debates.question: Is 'with joy' part of the original text?

sides.position: Probably a later insertion — absent from many best MSS, the passage grander without it

themes: none of these things move me
neither count I my life dear
finish my course and the ministry
the gospel of the grace of God

schools.summary: Paul declared the elders would see his face no more — largely agreed to be his expectation, not infallible foreknowledge: he spoke 'after the manner of men' taking his purpose for granted (Ellicott), and the Pastorals show he did revisit Ephesus (Robertson, 1 Timothy 1:3); the saying deepening the impression of his charge (Wesley).

debates.question: Did Paul certainly foreknow he would not see them again?

sides.position: It was his sincere expectation, not infallible prophecy — he spoke as men do, and events (the Pastorals) proved otherwise

themes: ye all shall see my face no more

schools.summary: Paul calls the elders to witness that he is pure from the blood of all men — the watchman's disclaimer (Ezekiel 33, Ellicott), guiltless of any man's destruction, having faithfully discharged his duty (Barnes).
Calvin: the pastor who fails to warn is guilty of the blood of the perishing — the awful responsibility of the office, faithfulness clearing the minister, negligence making him guilty of souls.

themes: I take you to record this day
pure from the blood of all men

schools.summary: Paul did not shun to declare all the counsel of God — not holding back from fear or favor (Chrysostom: silence incurs blood-guilt), but preaching the whole revealed will of God concerning salvation in Christ (Gill); the full proclamation the ground of his clear conscience (v.26).

themes: I have not shunned to declare all the counsel of God
the whole counsel, not a partial gospel

schools.summary: Paul charges the elders to feed the church of God purchased with his own blood; the text reads 'church of God' (Robertson, Ellicott, Wesley) or 'of the Lord' (Barnes, Clarke favor the older reading); 'overseers' (bishops) the same as elders, one office (Robertson); if 'God,' the deity of Christ is affirmed (Robertson cites Romans 9:5; Colossians 2:9).

Calvin: bishops differ nothing from elders; 'his own blood' explained by the communicatio idiomatum — blood attributed to God because the man Christ who shed it was also God, the unity of person against Nestorius and Eutyches; the church purchased with divine blood, to neglect it sacrilege.

debates.question: Does the text read 'church of God' or 'church of the Lord,' and what of 'his own blood'?

sides.position: 'Church of God,' with 'his own blood' = the blood of God, affirming Christ's deity (via the unity of his person)
'Church of the Lord' (the older reading), though Christ's deity stands either way

debates.question: Are 'overseers/bishops' a distinct order from elders?

sides.position: No — bishop and elder are one office, two names (the elders of v.17 called bishops here)

themes: take heed to yourselves and to all the flock
the Holy Ghost hath made you overseers
feed the church of God, purchased with his own blood
bishop=elder; the blood of God; the deity of Christ

schools.summary: Paul warns that after his departure grievous wolves — rapacious false teachers and persecutors (as Jesus foretold, John 10:12, Robertson) — will enter, not sparing the flock; his presence had restrained them, his absence would expose the church; fulfilled in the heresies and persecutions at Ephesus (the Pastorals, Revelation 2).
Calvin: the church is ever beset by wolves — a perennial danger requiring vigilant pastors; there is no time to sleep.

themes: after my departing shall grievous wolves enter

schools.summary: Paul warns that even from among the elders, men will arise speaking perverse things to draw away disciples — the most dangerous enemies nurtured within (Barnes), as later examples show (Hymenaeus, Philetus, Ellicott); seeking a following away from Christ.

Calvin: ambition, the desire for mastership, is the mother of all heresies — the pure ministry brings disciples to Christ alone, the corrupt seeks its own following, corrupting doctrine and dividing the church.

themes: of your own selves shall men arise
speaking perverse things, to draw away disciples

schools.summary: Paul charges the elders to watch (echoing Christ's command, Matthew 24:42, Ellicott — the overseers must be vigilant), remembering his own example: three years (his Ephesian ministry, roughly, Barnes) of ceaseless warning, night and day, with tears, to every individual; the model of pastoral diligence.

themes: three years I ceased not to warn every one

schools.summary: Paul commends the elders to God and the word of his grace — a consolation after the alarming warning (Chrysostom), grace being what saves and builds up; the word of grace able to build them up and give an inheritance among the sanctified (the hope of glory).
Chrysostom: not to overpower them after the warning of wolves, Paul gives consolation — commending them to God and his grace, putting them in mind of the hope to come.

themes: I commend you to God, and to the word of his grace

schools.summary: Paul disclaimed all covetousness — not merely that he had not taken, but that he had not even coveted silver, gold, or apparel (Chrysostom removing the root of evils, the love of money); his disinterestedness contrasting with false teachers who exploit the flock, vindicating his ministry.

themes: I have coveted no man's silver, or gold

schools.summary: Paul appeals to his manual labor — 'these hands' supporting himself and his companions (Chrysostom: toiling, to put them to shame), working at his tentmaking trade (18:3; 1 Corinthians 4:12) rather than burdening the church; the dignity of labor vindicating his disinterested ministry.

themes: these hands have ministered unto my necessities

schools.summary: Paul charges the elders, by his example of labor, to support the weak, and cites an unrecorded saying of Jesus (an agraphon, preserved only here): 'It is more blessed to give than to receive'; the saying expressing a counter-worldly truth — giving, not getting, is the higher blessedness, making us like God who gives to all (Henry).

themes: ye ought to support the weak
the words of the Lord Jesus
It is more blessed to give than to receive

schools.summary: Paul, having finished his charge, knelt and prayed with the elders — the fitting close of a parting (good for friends to part with prayer, Henry), a fervent prayer with much feeling (Chrysostom); the kneeling marking humble, earnest supplication, fulfilling the commendation of v.32.

themes: he kneeled down, and prayed with them all

schools.summary: The elders wept aloud, fell on Paul's neck, and kissed him — a scene of intense love and grief (such was their affection, Chrysostom), the embrace and kiss expressing deep affection in sorrowful parting; the fruit of Paul's loving ministry, the elders loving him as a father.

themes: they all wept sore, and fell on Paul's neck

schools.summary: The elders sorrowed most of all that they would see Paul's face no more (the personal loss grieving them more than the warnings of wolves, Chrysostom), and accompanied him to the ship — the final act of love, clinging to his presence to the last; the poignant close of the Miletus farewell.

themes: sorrowing most of all that they should see his face no more
the tender close of the farewell
